name: Login, Session & OAuth2/JWT
description: How modern web authentication works: cookie attributes, session vs token, JWT structure, the OAuth2 authorization-code flow, token refresh, keeping sessions alive, and the security checklist. From the login tutorial.

topics.name: Cookie Attributes
topics.description: HttpOnly, Secure, SameSite, Path/Domain, expiry, and the __Secure-/__Host- prefixes — what each one defends against.
topics.studyNotes:
Every attribute on a Set-Cookie header exists to close a specific attack, so learn them attack-first.

- HttpOnly — the cookie cannot be read from JavaScript (document.cookie). This is the XSS defence: injected script cannot steal the token.
- Secure — the cookie is only sent over HTTPS, never plain HTTP. Defends against eavesdropping (public wifi sniffing).
- SameSite — the CSRF defence. Strict: sent only from the same site, maximum protection. Lax (the default): sent on top-level GET navigations (clicking a link) but not on cross-site form POSTs. None: sent on every request including cross-site — allowed only together with Secure.
- Path — the cookie is only sent under the given path, e.g. Path=/api.
- Domain — which domains receive the cookie; unset means current host only, .myapp.com includes subdomains.
- Expires / Max-Age — automatic expiry: a concrete date vs seconds-to-live. With neither set, it is a session cookie that dies when the browser closes.
- Priority — Low/Medium/High: which cookies the browser evicts first when there are too many.
- Partitioned — the cookie lives in partitioned storage, the privacy mechanism for third-party cookies in newer browsers.

## Name prefixes

- __Secure- prefix: the browser only accepts the cookie if Secure is set.
- __Host- prefix: the strictest — requires Secure, Path=/, and NO Domain attribute, so the cookie cannot be scoped wider than the exact host.

A production-grade session cookie looks like: Set-Cookie: __Host-session=abc; Secure; Path=/; HttpOnly; SameSite=Strict.

Interview framing: do not just list flags — say what each one prevents. HttpOnly → XSS token theft; Secure → wire sniffing; SameSite → CSRF; __Host- → subdomain and path scoping tricks.

topics.name: Session vs Token Authentication
topics.description: Server-side sessions, stateless JWTs, and third-party OAuth2 — trade-offs in scaling, logout, and revocation.
topics.studyNotes:
Three approaches to "who is this request from?", each moving the state somewhere else.

## A) Server-side session (traditional)

On login the server creates a session record in memory or Redis (user id, login time, IP, user agent) and hands the client only a session ID in a cookie: Set-Cookie: session_id=abc123; HttpOnly; Secure; SameSite=Strict. The browser sends it automatically on every request; the server looks the session up.

- Pros: simple; logout is instant and server-side (delete the record); revocation is easy; CSRF is handled with SameSite.
- Cons: the server must store every session — scaling to multiple servers needs session sync or a shared store (Redis); session fixation is a risk; cross-domain use is awkward.

## B) Token-based (JWT)

The server signs a token containing everything it needs (user id, role, permissions, issued-at, expiry) and stores nothing. The client sends it in the Authorization: Bearer header; the server just verifies the signature and the exp claim.

- Pros: stateless and horizontally scalable — any server can verify; works cross-domain and is mobile-friendly (header, not cookie).
- Cons: logout is hard — the token stays valid until expiry unless you keep a blacklist, which reintroduces state; a stolen token grants access for its whole lifetime; the payload travels with every request.

## C) OAuth2 (third-party login)

The user authenticates at Google/GitHub; your backend exchanges the returned authorization code for tokens, then typically issues its own session or JWT. Scaling is easy and CSRF is covered by the state parameter.

The comparison interviewers want: where the data lives (server store / inside the token / at the provider), how each scales (sync needed / stateless / stateless), how logout works (delete session / blacklist / revoke+blacklist), and CSRF defence (SameSite cookie / X-CSRF-Token header / state parameter).

topics.name: JWT Structure & Storage
topics.description: header.payload.signature, standard vs custom claims, what the signature guarantees, and where to store the token on the client.
topics.studyNotes:
A JWT is three Base64 segments separated by dots: xxxxx.yyyyy.zzzzz.

- HEADER: the signing algorithm and type, e.g. {"alg": "HS256", "typ": "JWT"}.
- PAYLOAD: the claims. Standard claims from RFC 7519 — iat (issued at), exp (expires), nbf (not before), jti (token id, needed for revocation) — plus your custom claims (user_id, email, role, permissions).
- SIGNATURE: HMAC-SHA256(base64(header) + "." + base64(payload), secret_key) — or an RSA/asymmetric signature.

The critical mental model: header and payload are only Base64-encoded, NOT encrypted — anyone can decode and read them. Never put secrets in a JWT payload. What the signature gives you is integrity: only the server knows the secret key, so if anyone alters a claim the signature no longer matches. That is the guarantee the token is not forged.

The client may decode the payload (to read exp and schedule a refresh, or show the user's name), but signature verification can ONLY happen on the server — the client does not have the secret, and a client-side "check" proves nothing. The server must verify signature and expiry on every request.

## Where to store the token on the client

- localStorage: survives reloads, but any XSS can read it — the classic token-theft vector.
- HttpOnly + Secure + SameSite cookie: invisible to JavaScript, so XSS cannot exfiltrate it; needs CSRF thinking instead.
- Memory (state/Redux): safest against theft, but a page refresh loses it — behaves like a logout unless paired with a refresh mechanism.

Recommendation from the source: keep the JWT in an HttpOnly cookie; if you must use localStorage, keep access tokens short-lived and rely on a refresh-token strategy so a stolen token ages out quickly.

topics.name: OAuth2 Authorization Code Flow
topics.description: The full third-party login flow step by step, why the client secret never reaches the browser, and how OAuth2 relates to JWT.
topics.studyNotes:
The authorization code flow is the standard "Log in with Google" dance. The whole design revolves around one rule: the browser never sees the client secret.

- 1. The user clicks "Login with Google"; the frontend redirects to the provider's authorize endpoint with client_id, redirect_uri, scope, and a random state string.
- 2. The user authenticates at Google and approves the requested scopes (profile, email).
- 3. Google redirects back to your callback URL with an authorization code (and echoes state back — verify it!).
- 4. Your BACKEND posts the code + client_id + client_secret to the token endpoint. The secret lives only on the server.
- 5. Google validates and returns an ID token (user identity) and an access token.
- 6. The backend reads the user from the ID token, creates or updates the user record, issues its OWN session or JWT, and hands that to the frontend. From here the app works with its own token — Google is out of the loop.

The authorization code is single-use and short-lived: intercepting it is useless without the secret.

## OAuth2 vs JWT — not the same thing

OAuth2 is a protocol (who is the user, what may they access); JWT is a token format (signed data packaging). OAuth2 MAY use JWTs but does not have to: Google's access tokens are opaque strings (ya29...) that require a server-side lookup, while its ID tokens are JWTs. Modern providers (Auth0, Keycloak) often issue JWT access tokens signed asymmetrically — the provider signs with a private key and your backend verifies with the public key, no callback to the provider per request.

## Why the "OAuth2 login + own JWT" combo wins

Google handles login UX and credentials; your own JWT handles API access — verification is a local signature check instead of a Google API call per request, no provider rate limits, you control roles/scopes, logout is a local blacklist, and refresh is your policy.

Security points to say out loud: client_secret only on the backend; state parameter checked (CSRF); redirect URI whitelisted at the provider; HTTPS mandatory — an authorization code over HTTP is stealable.

topics.name: Token Refresh & Session Keep-Alive
topics.description: Short-lived access token + long-lived refresh token, rotation, device tracking, and the three keep-alive strategies.
topics.studyNotes:
The problem: access tokens are deliberately short-lived (15–60 minutes) so a stolen token has limited value — but users stay logged in for weeks without retyping passwords. The answer is a two-token system.

- Access token: 15–30 minutes. Sent with every API request. Lives in memory, localStorage, or a cookie.
- Refresh token: 7–30 days. Usable ONLY at the refresh endpoint, stored ONLY in an HttpOnly cookie (JavaScript cannot touch it), and tracked server-side so it can be revoked.

The flow the interviewer expects: an expired access token gets a 401 → the client calls POST /auth/refresh (the refresh cookie rides along automatically) → the server verifies the refresh token against its stored session (hashed — never store raw tokens), checks it is active, unexpired, and from the same device → issues a fresh access token → the client retries the original request. In a typical frontend this is an HTTP interceptor: catch the 401, refresh once, replay; if the refresh fails, log out.

Best practices from the source:
- Store refresh-token sessions in a database (hashed token, device fingerprint, IP, expiry) — this is what makes logout and "log out of all devices" possible.
- Token rotation: every refresh issues a new refresh token and invalidates the old one, so a stolen refresh token that gets used races the legitimate one and gets caught.
- Device fingerprinting: refresh only honoured from the device that created the session; a new device means logging in again.
- Logout = deactivate/delete the refresh session server-side AND clear the cookie. Client-only logout (deleting from localStorage) leaves a stolen token fully usable.

## Keeping the session alive

Three strategies when the user is present but idle:
- Silent refresh (heartbeat): refresh on a timer, e.g. every 10 minutes, in the background.
- Activity-based: on user interaction (click, keypress) check the decoded exp; if the token expires within ~2 minutes, refresh.
- Sliding window: every refresh issues a new full-length refresh token, so the session extends as long as the user keeps coming back — inactivity for the whole window logs them out naturally.

topics.name: Auth Security Best Practices
topics.description: The login-system checklist — HTTPS, hashing, rate limiting, CSRF defenses — and the classic mistakes.
topics.studyNotes:
The checklist for any login system, each item with its reason:

- HTTPS everywhere — a token is a password; over HTTP it can be sniffed.
- HttpOnly cookie for the refresh token — JavaScript (and therefore XSS) cannot reach it.
- Secure flag — cookies never travel over plain HTTP.
- SameSite=Strict — cookies only sent from your own site (CSRF defence).
- CORS configured to trusted origins only.
- Password hashing with bcrypt or argon2 — never MD5/SHA1, which are fast enough to brute-force.
- Rate limiting on login — e.g. max 5 attempts per 15 minutes against credential stuffing.
- Token expiry — access 15–60 minutes, refresh 7–30 days.
- Signature verification on the server for every single token.
- Logout invalidation — delete/deactivate the refresh session in the database.

## CSRF defences by auth style

- Cookie-based session: SameSite=Strict on the cookie.
- JWT in a header: an X-CSRF-Token custom header — cross-origin pages cannot set custom headers, so its presence proves same-origin.
- OAuth2: the state parameter, generated random, verified at the callback.

## Classic mistakes

- JWT in localStorage with XSS present — trivially stolen.
- Accepting tokens without verifying the signature — forged tokens walk in.
- Client-side-only logout — the server still honours the stolen token.
- OAuth2 over HTTP — authorization code and token theft.
- Refresh tokens that never expire — a stolen one is permanent access.
- No device tracking — a stolen refresh token works from anywhere.
- JWT secret reachable from the client — the whole scheme collapses; whoever has the secret can mint tokens.

Interview tip from the source: do not just recite the flow — attach the security reason to every choice. Why HttpOnly? Why short expiry? Why a refresh token at all? That reasoning is what is actually being scored.

questions.title: Session vs JWT
questions.prompt: What is the difference between server-side sessions and JWT-based authentication? When would you pick each?
questions.answerStructureHint: Where the state lives → scaling implications → logout/revocation difference → when each fits.

expectedPoints.label: Where the state lives
expectedPoints.description: Session: server stores the record (memory/Redis), cookie carries only an ID. JWT: all data in the token, server stateless.

expectedPoints.label: Scaling trade-off
expectedPoints.description: Sessions need sync or a shared store across servers; JWTs verify anywhere.

expectedPoints.label: Logout / revocation difference
expectedPoints.description: Session logout is instant (delete the record); JWT stays valid until expiry unless blacklisted.

followUps.prompt: A user's JWT is stolen. What can you actually do about it?

expectedPoints.label: Mitigating a stolen JWT
expectedPoints.description: Short expiry limits the damage; jti + blacklist or rotating the signing key revokes at a cost.

questions.title: Securing a session cookie
questions.prompt: You are setting the session cookie for a login system. Which cookie attributes do you set, and what does each one defend against?
questions.answerStructureHint: Attribute by attribute: flag → attack it prevents. Finish with the __Host- prefix as the strict default.

expectedPoints.label: HttpOnly against XSS
expectedPoints.description: Not readable from document.cookie, so injected script cannot steal it.

expectedPoints.label: Secure = HTTPS only
expectedPoints.description: Never sent over plain HTTP; defends against wifi sniffing.

expectedPoints.label: SameSite against CSRF
expectedPoints.description: Strict = same-site only; Lax allows top-level GET navigation; None requires Secure.

expectedPoints.label: Scope and expiry attributes
expectedPoints.description: Path/Domain scoping, Expires/Max-Age lifetime, __Secure-/__Host- prefixes for enforced strictness.

questions.title: What is inside a JWT
questions.prompt: Walk me through the structure of a JWT. What can the client safely do with it, and what must only happen on the server?

expectedPoints.label: Header.payload.signature
expectedPoints.description: Three dot-separated Base64 segments: algorithm, claims, signature.

expectedPoints.label: Readable, not encrypted
expectedPoints.description: Base64 is encoding, not encryption — anyone can read the payload; never put secrets in it.

expectedPoints.label: Signature = integrity guarantee
expectedPoints.description: Only the server knows the secret; altering a claim invalidates the signature — verification is server-only.

expectedPoints.label: Standard claims
expectedPoints.description: iat/exp/nbf/jti from RFC 7519; jti enables revocation.

followUps.prompt: Where do you store the JWT on the client, and what is the trade-off of each option?

expectedPoints.label: localStorage vs HttpOnly cookie vs memory
expectedPoints.description: localStorage is XSS-exposed; HttpOnly cookie is JS-invisible but needs CSRF care; memory dies on refresh.

questions.title: OAuth2 authorization code flow
questions.prompt: Explain the OAuth2 authorization code flow end to end — "Login with Google". Why does the browser never receive the client secret?
questions.answerStructureHint: Redirect with client_id+state → user consents → code to callback → backend exchanges code+secret for tokens → app issues its own token.

expectedPoints.label: Redirect with client_id, redirect_uri, scope, state
expectedPoints.description: The frontend sends the user to the provider's authorize endpoint.

expectedPoints.label: Backend exchanges the code with the secret
expectedPoints.description: The authorization code returns via the callback; only the backend trades code + client_secret for tokens.
acceptedSignals: client secret stays on the server

expectedPoints.label: Why the secret never reaches the browser
expectedPoints.description: Anything in the browser is readable; the code alone is useless without the secret, and it is single-use.
acceptedSignals: code is useless without the secret

expectedPoints.label: App issues its own token afterwards
expectedPoints.description: Backend reads identity from the ID token, creates the user, and issues its own JWT/session — Google is out of the loop.

followUps.prompt: Is OAuth2 the same thing as JWT?

expectedPoints.label: Protocol vs token format
expectedPoints.description: OAuth2 is an authorization protocol; JWT is a token format. OAuth2 may use JWTs (ID tokens) or opaque tokens.

questions.title: Why refresh tokens exist
questions.prompt: Why do we split authentication into an access token and a refresh token? Walk through what happens when the access token expires mid-session.

expectedPoints.label: Short access + long refresh
expectedPoints.description: Access: 15–30 min, used on every request — stolen means limited damage. Refresh: days/weeks, only for renewal.

expectedPoints.label: 401 → refresh → retry
expectedPoints.description: Expired access token returns 401; client calls the refresh endpoint (HttpOnly cookie rides along), gets a new access token, replays the request — invisible to the user.

expectedPoints.label: Refresh token protection
expectedPoints.description: HttpOnly cookie only, hashed server-side tracking, rotation on use, device fingerprint.

followUps.prompt: How do you keep the session alive while the user is active but idle between requests?

expectedPoints.label: Keep-alive strategies
expectedPoints.description: Silent refresh on a timer, activity-based refresh near expiry, or a sliding window extending on each refresh.

questions.title: CSRF defenses in modern login
questions.prompt: How do you defend against CSRF, and how does the defense differ between cookie-based sessions, JWT-in-header, and OAuth2?

expectedPoints.label: Understands the attack
expectedPoints.description: A malicious site triggers requests to your API riding on the browser's automatically-attached cookies.

expectedPoints.label: Sessions: SameSite cookie
expectedPoints.description: SameSite=Strict stops the cookie from accompanying cross-site requests.

expectedPoints.label: Headers and state parameter
expectedPoints.description: JWT: X-CSRF-Token custom header (cross-origin pages cannot set custom headers). OAuth2: random state verified at the callback.

questions.title: Login system security checklist
questions.prompt: You are reviewing a login implementation before it ships. What is on your security checklist, and which classic mistakes do you look for?
questions.answerStructureHint: Transport → storage → credentials → abuse limits → lifecycle (expiry, logout). Then the classic mistakes.

expectedPoints.label: HTTPS + hardened cookies
expectedPoints.description: HTTPS mandatory; HttpOnly, Secure, SameSite on auth cookies; CORS restricted to trusted origins.

expectedPoints.label: Password hashing + rate limiting
expectedPoints.description: bcrypt/argon2 (never MD5/SHA1); login attempts rate-limited against credential stuffing.

expectedPoints.label: Token lifecycle discipline
expectedPoints.description: Expiries set (access short, refresh bounded), signatures verified on every request, logout invalidates server-side.

expectedPoints.label: Spots the classic mistakes
expectedPoints.description: JWT in localStorage, client-only logout, never-expiring refresh tokens, secret reachable from the client.
